solution: Substitute \( u = \tan x \), so \( du = \sec^{2} x\,dx \) and the limits become \( u = 0 \) to \( u = \tan\tfrac{\pi}{4} = 1 \). Then \( [[c]]\int_{0}^{1} u^{[[n]]}\,du = \left.\tfrac{[[c]]\,u^{[[n]]+1}}{[[n]]+1}\right|_{0}^{1} = \tfrac{[[c]]}{[[n]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( [[n]] \) instead of \( [[n]]+1 \) (wrong power rule).
This divides by \( [[n]]+2 \), over-counting the exponent increase.
This multiplies by \( [[n]]+1 \) instead of dividing by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This triples the correct value.

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\arctan([[p]]/[[q]])} [[c]]\tan^{[[m]]} x\,\sec x\,dx \) (an odd power of tangent times a single secant factor).
solution: Substitute \( u = \sec x \), so \( du = \sec x\tan x\,dx \), and write \( \tan^{[[m]]}x = (\sec^{2}x-1)^{([[m]]-1)/2}\tan x \) since \( [[m]] \) is odd. Since \( \tan(\text{upper limit}) = \tfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \), \( \sec(\text{upper limit}) = \tfrac{\sqrt{[[p]]^{2}+[[q]]^{2}}}{[[q]]} \), and \( u \) runs from \( 1 \) to this value. The resulting polynomial integral in \( u \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( [[m]]+1 \), confusing this with the simple power-rule case.
This uses \( \tan(\text{bound}) \) directly instead of carrying out the \( u=\sec x \) substitution.
This uses only the endpoint value of \( \sec x \) times \( c \), skipping the actual integration.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/[[bidx]]} [[c]]\tan^{2} x\,dx \) (a pure power of tangent, using the identity \( \tan^{2}x = \sec^{2}x - 1 \)).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the \( \tan x \) antiderivative term and forgets to subtract \( x \).
This keeps only the \( -x \) term and forgets the \( \tan x \) antiderivative.
This adds the two antiderivative terms instead of subtracting them.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\arctan([[p]]/[[q]])} [[c]]\sec^{4} x\,dx \) (a pure even power of secant, using \( \sec^{2}x = 1+\tan^{2}x \) and \( u=\tan x \)).
solution: Write \( \sec^{4}x = \sec^{2}x(1+\tan^{2}x) \) and substitute \( u=\tan x \), \( du=\sec^{2}x\,dx \); since the upper limit is \( \arctan(\tfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}) \), \( u \) runs from \( 0 \) to \( \tfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \). Then \( [[c]]\int_{0}^{[[p]]/[[q]]}(1+u^{2})\,du = [[c]]\left(\tfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}+\tfrac{1}{3}\left(\tfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{3}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the \( \int 1\,du \) term and forgets the \( u^{2} \) contribution.
This doubles the \( u^{3}/3 \) term instead of applying the correct \( \tfrac{1}{3} \) factor.
This keeps only the \( \int u^{2}\,du \) term and forgets the constant contribution.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
